design_philosophy.core_loop: Collect cargo boxes (minimum 10 per level) to open level exit; navigate platforming hazards as Baloo or Kit
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource collection under platforming pressure—must gather sufficient cargo while overcoming level obstacles
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Join Baloo and Kit in aerial cargo delivery business, competing against Shere Khan's enterprise in a licensed Disney adventure
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Emphasizes cooperative gameplay (up to 2 players) and cargo collection as progression gating, tying mechanics directly to narrative (business competition). Character selection (Baloo vs Kit) suggests differentiated playstyles, though documentation is minimal.

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Two-character selection (Baloo/Kit) may offer difficulty variance, but specifics undocumented

progression.unlock_mechanism: Level exit unlocked by collecting minimum 10 cargo boxes per level
replayability_hooks: Two-player cooperative mode encourages shared playthroughs
Character selection (Baloo vs Kit) implies alternate routes/mechanics
Cargo collection quota suggests score-chasing potential

world_building_techniques: Licensed Disney IP (TaleSpin animated series) provides pre-existing world
Narrative framing: business competition between Rebecca's cargo service and Shere Khan's sabotage
Character roster (Baloo, Kit, Rebecca, Shere Khan, Don Karnage) embedded from source material

faction_indicators: Shere Khan's company (antagonist faction, economic threat)
Air pirates led by Don Karnage (hired antagonists)
danger_signals: Shere Khan's explicit goal to put Rebecca out of business
Air pirate interference in cargo delivery operations
Business competition framed as active sabotage
environmental_storytelling: Levels populated with cargo boxes as mechanical/narrative objects
Cape Suzette setting grounds story in specific location
Sky/aerial delivery context (Sea Duck vehicle in TG-16 bonus level) reinforces transportation theme
